policy_statement: Develop recipes to reduce emissions from City food services and protect the Baltic Sea.
mechanism_description: Recipes will be developed for the City’s food services to shift meals toward lower-emission options, reducing food-related greenhouse gas emissions while also supporting Baltic Sea protection.
primary_causal_pathway: Food procurement/menu choices → ↓ demand for high-emission foods → ↓ agricultural + supply-chain GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: City-operated food services (schools, hospitals, workplaces, etc.) redesign recipes and menus to substitute lower-emission ingredients/meals for higher-emission ones.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive agricultural production (especially enteric methane from ruminants, manure-related CH₄/N₂O, fertilizer-related N₂O) and reduced processing/transport emissions across the food supply chain.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower total consumption-based GHG emissions associated with municipal food service operations (primarily CH₄ and N₂O from agriculture, plus CO₂ from energy/transport).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions from food production and supply chains due to shifting menus toward lower-emission foods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Menu/procurement shifts reduce upstream food-system GHGs; the main effect is emissions reduction rather than efficiency, hazard reduction, or ecosystem restoration.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “resource efficiency” can be relevant (e.g., reducing food waste) and “nature-based” co-benefits can occur, the core mechanism here is dietary/procurement substitution to cut life-cycle emissions—i.e., mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented mainly as nutrient runoff reduction for Baltic Sea protection, the primary objective could appear environmental/water-quality; however the stated mechanism explicitly targets emissions reductions, and even when framed as co-benefits, the dominant climate effect remains GHG mitigation via demand shift.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that develops low-carbon recipes/menus for food services operates through the same demand/substitution channel to reduce food-system GHG emissions, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer classification (e.g., “methane reduction,” “consumption-based accounting,” “public procurement”) should be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific implementation details and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a particular program’s implemented changes are primarily to reduce eutrophication (e.g., phosphorus/nitrogen) with minimal actual dietary GHG shift, climate benefits could be secondary; otherwise straightforward mitigation via food-demand substitution.
key_indicators: “Recipes will be developed”; “reduce the emissions”; “City's food services”; “protect the Baltic Sea”
co_benefits: Reduced nutrient runoff/eutrophication impacts supporting Baltic Sea protection; potential public health benefits from dietary shifts
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement/menu standard if the “recipes” are later mandated/embedded in catering specifications, but the text only states development (guidance/planning) rather than a requirement.
